name: hardened_malloc
summary: GrapheneOS's 64-bit-only OpenBSD-malloc successor focused exclusively on hardening — heavy isolation, per-size-class regions with random bases, complete metadata/data separation, and double quarantine.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
GrapheneOS hardened_malloc is a 64-bit-only OpenBSD-malloc successor focused exclusively on hardening. It sits at the most-aggressive end of the security/throughput trade-off — accepting substantial overhead for maximum exploit mitigation. Where Scudo aims at practical mitigation that ships everywhere, hardened_malloc aims at the strongest defense possible for security-conscious deployments.

GrapheneOS uses it system-wide via Bionic; it is also usable on glibc/musl Linux as `LD_PRELOAD`.

sections.title: Out-of-band metadata and quarantine
sections.content:
All mutable allocator state lives in a single dedicated metadata region behind guard pages — no in-band metadata at all for slab allocations. Per-size-class regions have randomized base addresses; randomly sized guard pages around each large allocation make equal-sized requests yield non-deterministic mappings. Double quarantine combines a randomized array and a queue: freed blocks sit out-of-circulation long enough that adjacent-allocation reuse attacks lose their timing window, and write-after-free detection clears blocks on free so subsequent dangling writes are caught.

Integrates with ARM MTE on supported devices for hardware-tagged allocation on top of the address-space hardening.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): system-wide allocator on GrapheneOS via Bionic; available as `LD_PRELOAD` on glibc/musl Linux. The threat model is high-value mobile users (journalists, activists, researchers); the substantial overhead is justified by the exploit-mitigation surface area it removes.
